questions.stem: What is the key insight of the 'verification continuum' presented in class?
options: Verification is binary: a response is either fully verified or it is not
Verification methods provide increasing evidence of correctness, and higher-confidence methods usually cost more
Only mathematical proof counts as verification; everything else is just testing
All verification methods yield similar confidence, so you should always pick the cheapest one
questions.explanation: Unless it is a mathematical proof, verification is not binary: methods from LLM-as-a-Judge through self-consistency, MAD, validators, and formal verification give increasing evidence of correctness, and methods that give greater confidence typically cost more in time, effort, or money.

questions.stem: In the four levels of verification, running tests, validators, and compilation on an LLM's output belongs to which level?
options: Input verification
Output verification
Execution verification
Formal verification
questions.explanation: Execution verification answers 'Does it actually work?' using tests, validators, and compilation. Output verification only asks whether the answer looks reasonable (LLM judge, majority voting, MAD), which is a weaker check.

questions.stem: Your team refines prompts with prompt engineering, adds RAG context, and screens requests with input guardrails before they reach the LLM. In the four-level verification model, these measures address which question?
options: Did we ask the right question?
Does the answer look reasonable?
Does it actually work?
Can we prove correctness?
questions.explanation: Prompt engineering, RAG, and input guardrails are the lecture's examples of input verification, whose question is 'Did we ask the right question?'. The other questions correspond to output, execution, and formal verification.

questions.stem: In the LLM-as-a-Judge technique, verification is performed by:
options: Running the response through a rule-based validator that checks for required keywords
Executing the response against a suite of unit tests
Asking the end user to rate the response before it is accepted
Giving the prompt and response to another LLM that decides whether the response is correct
questions.explanation: In LLM-as-a-Judge, the prompt and its response (or several competing responses) are handed to a judging LLM that decides correctness. Validators, tests, and user ratings are different verification techniques.

questions.stem: How does self-consistency ENHANCE plain majority voting?
options: It asks a separate judge LLM to score each response before votes are counted
It adds Chain-of-Thought prompting so an answer reached through different reasoning paths can be trusted more
It weights each vote by the model's self-reported confidence in its answer
It lowers the temperature to zero so every sample returns the same answer
questions.explanation: Self-consistency uses CoT prompting with examples so responses expose their reasoning, then accepts the most frequent answer, assuming a result inferred by different reasoning paths is more likely correct. It involves no judge, confidence weighting, or deterministic sampling.

questions.stem: The paper 'Correlated Errors in Large Language Models' weakens the case for majority voting by showing that:
options: When two models both err, they agree on the same wrong answer about 60% of the time on one leaderboard dataset
Majority voting only helps when more than ten models participate in the vote
Models almost never agree with each other, so no majority can form in practice
Larger models always outvote smaller ones, biasing results toward a single vendor
questions.explanation: The paper found substantial correlation in model errors — on one leaderboard dataset models agree 60% of the time when both err. Agreement can therefore indicate a shared mistake rather than a correct answer, undercutting the core assumption of majority voting.

questions.stem: Self-critique (also called self-reflection or self-verification) follows which three-step loop?
options: Plan the solution, execute it, ask a human to approve the result
Generate multiple results, vote among them, return the winner
Generate a result, find flaws in the result, repair the answer
Retrieve references, generate a result, cite the references
questions.explanation: Self-critique generates a result, finds flaws in it, and repairs the answer — the pattern used, for example, when repairing generated code that fails unit tests. Voting and retrieval belong to other techniques.

questions.stem: A developer loops an LLM: generate an answer, then 'review your previous answer and produce feedback', then re-answer using that feedback — with no external signals such as test results. Research on self-correction suggests this will:
options: Reliably converge to the correct answer after a few iterations
Improve accuracy, but only if the temperature is raised each round
Match the gains of majority voting at a fraction of the cost
Often fail to help, and in many cases performance even deteriorates
questions.explanation: 'Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet' found that in exactly this setting — no additional external feedback — performance after self-correction often deteriorates. Self-critique works far better when grounded in external signals such as failing tests.

questions.stem: In the Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) example of analyzing a potential phishing email, which component defines the RULES the debate must follow?
options: The affirmative-role agent, which opens the debate
The judge, which evaluates the agents' arguments
An interaction protocol defined for the debate
The negative-role agent, which challenges claims
questions.explanation: In MAD, one agent takes the 'affirmative' role and one the 'negative', a judge manages the process and evaluates the arguments, and a separate interaction protocol governs the rules of the debate.

questions.stem: You use an LLM judge to pick the better of two candidate answers. Swapping the order in which the candidates are presented frequently flips the verdict, even though their content is unchanged. This is an instance of:
options: Verbosity bias
Positional bias
Self-enhancement bias
Sampling randomness due to a high temperature setting
questions.explanation: Position bias — the placement of arguments influencing the judgment — is a documented LLM-judge weakness that varies across judges and tasks. Verbosity bias favors longer answers and self-enhancement bias favors the judge's own outputs; a systematic flip on reordering is not random noise.

questions.stem: Which statement BEST captures the role of RAG with respect to verification?
options: It mainly improves generation by supplying reference documents, and can also verify by checking the answer is supported by them
It replaces verification entirely, since retrieved documents are always authoritative
It only verifies answers after generation and has no effect on answer quality
It is purely an input guardrail that filters out ambiguous user prompts
questions.explanation: RAG primarily works before/during generation by providing reference documents that improve accuracy, but it can also serve verification by checking that the produced solution is supported by those reference documents.

questions.stem: An LLM in your pipeline generates SQL queries for analysts. Which is the CHEAPEST property-validator style check to increase confidence before the queries run?
options: Formally prove each query equivalent to a specification using an SMT solver
Run a SQL validator that checks the query has proper syntax
Ask the same LLM whether its own query looks correct
Have a human DBA manually review every generated query
questions.explanation: Property validators cheaply check properties of the result — the lecture's first example is a SQL query validator that checks proper syntax. Formal proof and human review cost far more, and unaided self-review by the same model is unreliable.

questions.stem: An agentic delivery system repeatedly fails to find the right drop-off location after several tries, and a human operator is then called in to complete the task. In the human-in-the-middle taxonomy taught in class, this is an example of:
options: Human validation of the agent's final result
Providing a human touch at a fixed point in the flow
Human labeling of training data for the next model version
The human taking control when the LLM agent has difficulty
questions.explanation: The lecture lists three human-in-the-middle patterns: human validation of a produced result, providing a human touch at a specific point (e.g., an angry customer), and taking control when the agent keeps failing — the delivery-location example belongs to the third case.

questions.stem: Blocking prompt-injection attempts before a request ever reaches the model is an example of:
options: An input guardrail
An output guardrail
A property validator
Constrained decoding
questions.explanation: Input guardrails protect the LLM from inappropriate or manipulative prompts — screening harmful inputs, resolving ambiguity, and blocking prompt injections. Output guardrails act on the model's responses instead.

questions.stem: A team adds a strict JSON-schema guardrail to its code-generation service and concludes that the emitted code is now bug-free. What is wrong with this conclusion?
options: JSON-schema guardrails only work with closed-source models
Guardrails enforce format and policy well, but generally cannot verify algorithmic correctness or bug-free code
Guardrails slow generation so much that they cannot run in production
Schema guardrails can validate XML output but not JSON output
questions.explanation: Guardrails excel at enforcing well-formedness — JSON/XML schemas, regex, CFG/EBNF, constrained decoding — but they cannot verify algorithm correctness, factual correctness, architectural soundness, bug-free code, or requirement satisfaction.

questions.stem: In a RAIL specification (used by Guardrails AI), which component is used to implement custom validators and custom corrective actions?
options: Output
Prompt
Schema
Script
questions.explanation: RAIL is an XML dialect with three components: Output (structure, field types, quality criteria, and corrective actions for the expected response), Prompt (the template), and the optional Script, which holds custom code such as custom validators and corrective actions.

questions.stem: In Guardrails AI, what does Guard().use(ValidJson, on_fail="exception") set up?
options: A guard that checks the value parses as valid JSON and raises an exception when validation fails
A guard that automatically reformats any invalid JSON into valid JSON
A guard that limits the LLM response to a single line of JSON
A guard that installs the validator from the guardrails hub at runtime
questions.explanation: ValidJson is a pre-built validator that checks the value is parseable as JSON — for example, a trailing comma triggers a validation failure — and on_fail="exception" selects throwing an exception as the failure action. Installation is a separate 'guardrails hub install' command.

questions.stem: What is constrained decoding?
options: Fine-tuning the model so that it only sees valid outputs during training
Post-processing a finished response to delete any tokens that violate the schema
Restricting next-token sampling so only tokens valid under the supplied constraint (e.g., a schema) can be generated
Lowering the temperature so the model deterministically picks the most likely token
questions.explanation: By default a model may sample any token, which lets it break formats like JSON. Constrained decoding restricts sampling at each step to tokens that are valid under the supplied schema — it is not post-hoc filtering, fine-tuning, or temperature control.

questions.stem: How can a finite-state machine (FSM) be used in constrained decoding to guarantee valid JSON output?
options: Tokens that would violate the FSM are assigned probability 0, so the LLM never selects them
The FSM rewrites the finished response into the nearest valid JSON string
The FSM re-ranks whole candidate responses after generation completes
The FSM replaces the LLM and generates the JSON structure directly
questions.explanation: Constraints can be specified via finite-state machines, context-free/context-sensitive grammars, or global/logical constraints; potential tokens that violate the FSM (or CFG) are given probability 0 during decoding and therefore cannot be chosen.

questions.stem: In grammar-constrained decoding, what happens at EACH token-generation step?
options: The full grammar derivation is completed first, and then tokens are filled in
A single valid token is computed deterministically from the parser state
Every candidate token is checked with the parser; valid ones form a whitelist, and the next token is sampled from that whitelist
Tokens are sampled freely, and the sentence is re-parsed only after generation completes
questions.explanation: The algorithm gets the probability distribution over next tokens, checks for each candidate whether the partial sentence plus that token is still valid per the parser, whitelists the valid tokens, and samples from the whitelist — sampling stays probabilistic among the valid tokens.

questions.stem: You ask an LLM to classify requirements as 'F' (functional) or 'NF' (non-functional), but it keeps returning explanations and variants like 'Functional requirement'. Which technique FORCES the model to output only one of the two labels?
options: Adding a length validator that rejects long answers after generation
Constrained decoding with the fixed label set
Raising the frequency penalty so extra words become less likely
Using majority voting over several sampled classifications
questions.explanation: Classification with a fixed label set is one of the lecture's constrained-decoding scenarios: CD guarantees only the defined labels can be emitted. A validator merely rejects bad output after the fact, and penalties or voting cannot guarantee the format.

questions.stem: In formal program verification, a loop invariant is a condition that must hold:
options: Only at the exact moment the loop terminates
Only immediately before the loop begins executing
For every input that also satisfies the postcondition
Before the loop begins, at the start of each iteration, and after the loop terminates
questions.explanation: A loop invariant must hold before the loop begins, at the start of each iteration, and after the loop terminates. Preconditions constrain what holds before a function runs, and postconditions what it guarantees afterwards.

questions.stem: In deductive verification (as used by Dafny in Clover), how is a program proven correct with respect to its specification?
options: The program and its annotations are converted into verification conditions that an SMT solver checks are valid for all inputs
The program is run on a large randomized test suite until coverage reaches 100%
An LLM judge compares the program's observed behavior with the specification text
A model checker exhaustively executes all inputs up to a fixed bound
questions.explanation: A deductive verifier converts the program plus its preconditions, postconditions, and loop invariants into logical verification conditions, and an SMT solver checks that they are valid — true for all possible inputs — proving correctness with respect to the specification.

questions.stem: You ask an LLM to write a function and also to write the formal annotations for it. The deductive verifier passes, yet the function does not do what you wanted. According to the lecture, the MOST likely cause is:
options: The SMT solver timed out and reported success by default
Dafny is unable to check loop invariants that were generated by LLMs
The LLM wrote annotations consistent with its own incorrect code, so the proof verified the wrong specification
The precondition was too strong, so no input could ever exercise the bug
questions.explanation: When the same LLM writes both the code and the annotations, it often writes annotations that match the incorrect code — the verifier passes, but the code is wrong with respect to what the user really wanted. This is the failure mode Clover's cross-artifact consistency checks target.

questions.stem: Clover's consistency checking is built around which three artifacts?
options: Source code, unit tests, and code-review comments
A natural-language docstring, the code, and formal annotations (the pre/postconditions and invariants)
A prompt, a retrieved reference document, and the model's answer
Requirements documents, architecture diagrams, and the implementation
questions.explanation: Clover requires mutual consistency among the docstring (natural-language intent), the code, and the annotations (formal spec), defining six consistency checks — two for each pair of artifacts.

questions.stem: Why does Clover include the Annotations-to-Code check, in which an LLM regenerates code from the annotations and checks equivalence with the original code?
options: To ensure the annotations are strong enough — trivial annotations would not contain enough information to reconstruct equivalent code
To speed up Dafny by giving it two candidate programs to verify in parallel
To translate the program into a second language for portability testing
To confirm the docstring matches the code without needing a verifier
questions.explanation: Code-to-Annotations is standard formal verification, but it would accept trivially weak annotations. The reverse check tests annotation completeness: if an LLM can regenerate functionally equivalent code from the annotations alone, they capture the full functionality.

questions.stem: The 'LLM bootstrap problem' described in the lecture refers to:
options: Starting an agent without any system prompt or tools configured
Getting an LLM to generate artifacts in a data-poor language (e.g., Verus) that has too few public examples to learn from
The first generated token constraining all of the subsequent tokens
Loading a formal verifier into memory before the LLM finishes generating code
questions.explanation: The bootstrap problem is educating an LLM to perform well in a data-poor language: Verus, a verification language for Rust, has fewer than 10 public repos, while Dafny has 10 years of accumulated data. AlphaVerus addresses this by translating from the resource-rich domain.

questions.stem: In AlphaVerus, the Treefinement phase:
options: Translates Dafny programs into Verus programs using a few-shot prompt
Uses rule-based, comparison, and exploit models to detect flawed specifications
Runs a tree search over candidate fixes to partially verified programs, guided by the verifier's error messages
Adds newly verified programs and error trajectories to the data pool for the next iteration
questions.explanation: Exploration translates Dafny to Verus, Treefinement tree-searches over fixes to partially verified programs using verifier errors and a scoring function that balances exploitation and exploration, and Critique detects flawed specs; the data-pool update is the separate self-improvement loop.

questions.stem: A small startup needs its LLM to emit machine-readable output for downstream code, on a minimal budget. According to the verification-ROI table, which technique offers a HUGE benefit at the lowest cost?
options: Integration tests
Formal verification
Self-consistency
JSON schema validation
questions.explanation: The ROI table rates JSON schema as tiny cost with huge benefit (constrained decoding is low cost/huge benefit). Self-consistency is medium/medium, integration tests are high cost, and formal verification is very high cost with application-dependent benefit.

questions.stem: According to the lecture on the economics of verification, verification is valuable when:
options: The verification technique is fully automated
The expected cost of failure exceeds the cost of obtaining additional evidence
The application produces structured rather than free-form output
At least two independent verification methods are available
questions.explanation: Verification costs time, effort, and money; it pays off when the expected cost of failure exceeds the cost of obtaining additional evidence. The likelihood of a mistake, often measurable empirically, also enters the decision.

questions.stem: Based on classical software-engineering experience cited in class, in which situation does FORMAL verification most clearly pay for itself?
options: An avionics component where failures are extremely expensive and the specification is stable
A marketing-copy generator that is refreshed for each new campaign
A prototype chatbot whose requirements change every week
A logging utility where mistakes are cheap and easily fixed
questions.explanation: Formal verification pays when failures are extremely expensive, specifications are stable, and the cost can be amortized — classic examples are cryptography, distributed protocols, hardware, avionics, and medical equipment. Cheap-mistake or fast-changing applications fail these criteria.

questions.stem: Your LLM-generated domain code passes its unit tests, yet errors keep surfacing later in production. Which technique would BEST help catch these escaped defects?
options: Raise the LLM's temperature so it produces more varied code
Regenerate the code until it passes the same test suite on the first try
Have another LLM generate additional unit tests for the code and run them against it
Shorten the prompt so the model focuses only on the core logic
questions.explanation: If code passes its tests but fails later, the test suite is too weak — having a second LLM generate more unit tests (or adding static analysis) strengthens execution verification. Temperature, prompt length, and re-passing the same suite do nothing about coverage.

questions.stem: Self-consistency is based upon the idea that LLMs should mimic people by:
options: Placing greater trust in an answer that many arrive at, but for different reasons
Thinking silently before answering a question
Studying a subject thoroughly before answering questions about it
Illustrating complicated answers with concrete examples
questions.explanation: Self-consistency samples multiple reasoning paths and accepts the most frequent answer, mirroring how people trust a conclusion more when many reach it independently for different reasons. Merely thinking first is plain CoT, not the consistency idea.

questions.stem: You are building a solution that must generate both code and unit tests for that code. Following the multi-agent design guidance reviewed in the course, it is best to:
options: Have one agent do both, since it best understands its own code
Generate the tests first and then never revise them afterwards
Let the code-writing agent grade its own tests to save tokens
Separate code generation and test generation into distinct agents so each focuses on one task
questions.explanation: The course's collaborative-agents guidance is to separate code generation and test generation into their own agents so each can focus on a single task — an agent testing its own code inherits the same blind spots that produced the bugs.

questions.stem: An LLM scores very high on a code-generation benchmark even though its solutions fail every unit test. Which scoring metric could produce this outcome?
options: pass@k, which rewards any syntactically valid sample
CodeBLEU, which measures similarity to a reference solution rather than test execution
Test-case average, which averages the fraction of passing tests per problem
pass@1, computed over a single greedy sample per problem
questions.explanation: CodeBLEU scores token overlap, AST similarity, and dataflow similarity against a canonical solution, so code can resemble the reference while failing every test. pass@k, pass@1, and test-case average all depend on tests actually passing and would be near zero.

questions.stem: Your customer-complaint assistant performs well on an industry-standard benchmark but poorly once deployed to production. The MOST likely explanation for this discrepancy is:
options: The model's context window is too small for customer queries
The LLM's sampling parameters are badly tuned
The chosen model is too small for language-heavy tasks
The benchmark is not representative of the queries the system receives in practice
questions.explanation: Public benchmarks are fixed, general datasets built to compare models broadly; if production queries differ from the benchmark distribution, high benchmark scores will not transfer. That is why you build custom evals for your specific use case and run them continuously.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly contrasts EVALS with public benchmarks?
options: Evals are fixed public datasets, while benchmarks are custom-built per application
Benchmarks are run continuously in production, while evals are usually run once
Evals are custom to your app and run continuously for regression testing, while benchmarks are fixed, public, and compare models broadly
Benchmarks measure business metrics, while evals measure only token-level accuracy
questions.explanation: Per the recitation's comparison table: benchmarks are created by researchers on fixed public datasets to compare models broadly and are often run once, while evals are built by your team for your specific use case and run continuously as regression tests.

questions.stem: Which of the following is listed in the course as a COMMON MISTAKE in LLM evaluation?
options: Building a custom eval set instead of reusing a public benchmark
Running regression tests every time the prompt or model changes
Measuring safety and bias in addition to answer accuracy
Using public benchmarks as validation for your specific product
questions.explanation: The listed mistakes are: using benchmarks as product validation, using accuracy when the task is subjective, not testing edge cases, and overfitting to the eval set. The other options describe recommended practices, not mistakes.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a course-endorsed way to increase your confidence in an LLM's response?
options: Raise the temperature so the model explores more diverse answers
Ask the same model to simply re-answer with no new information
Validate the response using an external tool
Increase the max-tokens limit so the answer is more complete
questions.explanation: Validating with an external tool, majority voting across LLMs, and critique by another LLM all increase confidence in a response. Re-answering without any external feedback is intrinsic self-correction, which research shows can even degrade performance.
